data.title: Amer Sports: Solid Performance That Dismissed My Bearish Concerns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Amer Sports (AS) was a hold rating because I was still afraid of the risks associated with the Tibet backlash in China, insider selling trends, and the high valuation. After looking at the Q3 numbers, the setup for AS has gotten a lot better. It didn't just post a strong print; it dismissed the key risks I was worried about. With China accelerating and Arc'teryx accelerating, the fundamentals look far cleaner today. I upgrade to buy.

3Q25 Results Update

AS delivered a solid Q3 that dismissed my bearish view previously. Revenue came in at $1.76 billion, up 30% y/y (28% in constant currency), and well ahead of consensus. Every segment and region contributed to this growth, which is good since growth is not driven by a single brand. Outdoor Performance was the outperformer, up 36% y/y to $724 million, and Technical Apparel also did well, growing 31% y/y to $683 million. Even Ball & Racquet grew 16% y/y to $350 million. Adj. gross margin expanded 240 bps to 57.9%, which drove adj. EBIT growth of 41% y/y to $275 million (EBIT margins came in at 15.7%). Bottom line, adj. EPS more than doubled to $0.33, and this solid performance led management to raise guidance across the board, now calling for 23–24% revenue growth and $0.88–$0.92 in adj. EPS.

China Growth Accelerated

The biggest surprise in Q3 was how fast the “fireworks incident” faded off the radar. In my last update, I pointed out the risk that the online backlash in China could hit demand, especially given that Greater China is AS's most profitable region. Fortunately, the numbers came in way better than expected. Growth in Greater China actually accelerated to 47% y/y, up from 42% in Q2. For one, this tells us that Arc'teryx and Salomon brand equity are far stronger than I expected; and two, it also shows that demand is likely structural (like real organic demand) that is not sentiment driven (which could easily disappear whenever sentiment turns negative).

My take is that demand momentum has continued into Q4, and there are two data points to support my view. Firstly, management noted that while there was some softness in early October because of warm weather and the incident, the trends have already “rebounded.” Secondly, the fact that management didn’t bake any negative impact into their Q4 guide (and even explicitly said strong momentum continues) is a great hint that momentum continues (the earnings call was in November, so they basically know the first 5 to 6 weeks of Q4 have performed). For the bull equity story, this is a major win. It shows that Chinese consumers are still demanding these brands despite the macro weakness.

Arc'teryx Omni-Comp Re-Accelerated Significantly

The other concern was whether Arc'teryx could continue to post strong growth as the base got larger. Well, it certainly seems like growth is not slowing down, as Technical Apparel omni-comp sales (which include DTC stores and e-commerce) growth accelerated to 27% in Q3 2025, 12 points above Q2. If growth accelerated by this much, it is as clear as any data can get that underlying demand remains strong and also that the broader “brand billboard” rollout is working (refer to the quote from my previous update below for details). Also, since most of the comp sales growth strength came from traffic (as per management), not ticket or conversion, it further supports the narrative that more people are discovering the brand and showing up in stores.

The story gets better if you consider that markdown levels were unchanged y/y. Basically, this means that the sharp acceleration in growth came from full-price demand, not discounts (further supporting the strong narrative that organic demand is strong). This confirms that Arc'teryx isn't “buying” growth via discounts (which is not sustainable).

The idea is to roughly double Arc'teryx's global store footprint from ~168 locations in 2025 to ~300 by 2030, with a longer-term target of 500–600. Besides the obvious fact that these stores act as distribution, these stores will also act as “brand billboards” that help drive awareness.

I believe this strategy could work because the physical stores are designed to create a "halo" effect that drives more sales in a new market, and not just the sales within that store's four walls. The increased visibility and local presence should also funnel consumers to the e-commerce business, and with higher consumer mindshare and demand, it should also lead to a lift in demand from wholesale partners in the region as well. Therefore, each new store should be seen as a lever that drives demand across multiple channels.

Initial FY 2026 Outlook and Valuation

The initial guide for FY2026 should put to rest the idea that AS growth momentum is slowing. In contrast to that, management guided revenue growth toward the high end of their long-term guide (low double-digit to mid-teens, with 30–70 bps of EBIT margin expansion). I actually think that guide is too conservative, especially when considering that Q3 saw every region accelerate, and Greater China is growing by ~50% y/y. Elsewhere, the Americas also accelerated to 18% y/y in Q3 2025 (vs. 6% in Q2), which is really telling of the brand strength since the consumer demand backdrop is not great in the US. If AS can grow in the high teens in this environment, when rates (suppose the Fed cuts) come down next year (which should help with consumer demand), demand should easily be sustained in the high teens.

All of these matter for the stock because at ~31x forward PE, AS needs to prove it can continue to compound earnings at a high rate, and this guide de-risks that growth outlook. It was also very positive to hear management say that they see no evidence of a slowdown. My bottom line here is that with a solid setup for earnings to compound at >25% for the next few years (if momentum holds, or overall macro conditions get better), the current multiple isn't too stretched. In fact, I could argue it is cheap since Nike trades at 31x forward PE but only has a growth expectation of mid-single-digits.

Risk

I am optimistic that the Q4 guide is a deliberate conservative action by management. But if it's not, then the stock could see heavy selling pressure, as the implied Q4 guide suggests revenue growth decelerating to 17-20% y/y, a drop from Q3's 30% y/y. Inventory also remains elevated (up 28% y/y), so if demand really falls, we could be in a situation where AS needs to start marking down, and that will not be pretty.

Conclusion

AS now has a credible path to compounding earnings at a high-teens to >20% pace, and the early FY26 guide suggests that momentum isn't fading. The China concern I had was also a non-event, and Arc'teryx is also accelerating off a larger base. At ~31x forward P/E, the stock could be argued to be cheap when compared to Nike, which has lower growth but similar multiples. I upgrade to buy.

data.title: HYBI: An Adequate Hold For Income
data.description:
NEOS Enhanced Income Credit Select ETF (HYBI) offers a balance between "junk" bonds and options writing for income. With a year on its belt, it's not done badly, but it also seems like it could disappoint under duress, for which it remains untested.

Concept of the Fund

HYBI explicitly aims to be a "fund of funds," achieving its investment goals not by selecting or filtering for individual investments but by investing in other funds. As they state in the prospectus:

Although the Fund may invest directly in debt securities, it will primarily invest in such securities indirectly through ETFs that each invest primarily in debt securities.

HYBI primarily invests in debt through its ETF holdings. The prospectus goes on to elaborate that they are open to investment-grade and "junk" debt alike, while also not seeking a target maturity or duration.

In addition to the income from its debt allocation, the fund will also write SPX put spreads for additional income. They may also rotate or reposition into debt that may appreciate from a catalyst (as a high yield might from a rate cut by a central bank).

The recent holdings above show that they largely lean into three funds currently, each with weightings just above 30%:

  • iShares Broad USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (USHY)
  • Xtrackers USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYLB)
  • State Street SPDR Portfolio High Yield Bond ETF (SPHY)

A look back to February shows these same funds, with much greater concentration in USHY compared to the other two. This reflects the fund's willingness to rebalance based on which of the funds they find favorable at the time.

Historical Results

HYBI has just over a year of results, but it is at least some time to assess how it's delivered on its income mission.

Incepted in September 2024, HYBI has paid monthly dividends since that October. Distributions have varied between $0.40 at the high end and $0.32 at the low. Overall, it's worked out to a yield between 8% and 9%.

Since its launch, the share price is down by a few percent, most of this occurring in late March and early April, related to market fears about President Trump's Liberation Day and tariff policy, which affected debt in addition to equities.

Overall, it's been meeting its income goal, without permanent and serious impacts to the principal.

Outlook and Risks

The first question we might ask is if it's worth it to incur the indirect management fees of the holdings, while also directly paying the ones due to HYBI.

  • USHY: 0.08%
  • HYLB: 0.05%
  • SPHY: 0.05%

These are all less than 10 BPS, which isn't much. Meanwhile, HYBI's official page mentions that the net expense ratio, counting those in the holdings, is capped at 0.68%. The annual report mentioned this initially expired in December, so I think we're seeing some commitment by the fund to maintain that bottom line. Whether or not investors like that 68 BPS is the real question, but it's not so high that I'd rule out HYBI on that alone.

If HYBI can continue to yield 8% to 9%, it may be worth it for the income. Again, let's look at the yields of the components and how they earn them.

  • USHY: 6.8%
  • HYLB: 6.29%
  • SPHY: 7.39%

USHY invests directly in corporate bonds, concentrated on maturities of 3 to 5 years, across many sectors, and below investment-grade. It's very diversified, with nearly every issuer consisting of less than a percent of the holdings.

HYLB is similar with its concentration in "junk" bonds, with heavy diversification to reduce individual exposure to less than 1% and a slight preference for consumer and communication sectors.

SPHY is similar to both funds in these ways as well. If it differs, it's that its bonds lean more toward longer maturities by a couple of years, but it's not a huge difference.

Overall, a bet on HYBI is a bet that these bond funds will yield about 6.5%, supported by another 1.5% or so from the SPX put spreads.

As the bonds are largely BB or B credit ratings, and as a put spread generally makes money in a bull market, the main risk would be macro weakness that could lead to defaults on below-investment issuers, as well as unprofitable results on the put trades. In other words, it's a fund that should do well if the market does well. The debt structure of the investments may give HYBI more protection than equities on the downside, but it wouldn't rescue it in a crunch.

Let's remember how disruptive Trump's tariffs seemed back in April. It's not surprising that HYBI declined as it did, as lower credit tiers would have been more affected. Investors in HYBI mostly have to think that the income will pull through. While the strategy allows for reallocation into investment-grade bond funds, those aren't the current holdings, and so that isn't the current offer. Anyone looking for capital gains from "flight to safety" would probably need to look elsewhere.

HYBI can, like any bond fund, capture gains from additional rate cuts by the Fed, which did happen recently. I'm not expecting those to be cut nearly as quickly as they were hiked in the first place, however.

Conclusion

HYBI seems like a bit of a mixed bag. The yield is high, which is good, but the underlying portfolio seems to prefer riskier bonds. Short of a double-digit yield, I wouldn't want HYBI for income. Lacking strong drivers of capital gains to make up some of that difference, I am not seeing a clear advantage to warrant a position. For those simple reasons, I think it's an adequate hold.

data.title: Pool Corporation: A Long-Term Buy Hidden Behind Near-Term Stagnation
data.description:
I see good value in a long-term Buy thesis in Pool Corporation (POOL) at current valuations. And this is not a simple case of bottom-fishing at its near-5-year lows. I see very good execution in terms of inventory levels, margin maintenance, and competitive position. The revenue stagnation and soft demand macro should eventually play out favorably for patient investors and reward fresh investments made today.

Nuanced Take on Revenue Stagnation

Pool's revenue stagnation since 2022-23 has been near-generational. Last such dip was seen in the 2008-09 recession. There are several factors that make this dip more interesting. For once, what started as demand softness due to higher rates in 2022-23 has lasted longer, as despite rate cuts in the works, consumer spending and economic growth outlook still remain hazy. Consumer spending has been working out relatively well in some cohorts like essentials and groceries (as expected). Interestingly, some reports suggest higher earnings groups are still generating enough discretionary income that is supportive in some pockets of travel, leisure, and even gaming. However, Pool's swimming pool buildouts and renovations have a wider macro base and suffer a bit due to the non-essentials positioning.

Because of Pool's positioning, a revenue rebound is not visible yet, as economic uncertainties will outweigh even further rate cuts - particularly important for new pool constructions. The good news for Pool is that the bulk of its revenues come from maintenance and minor repairs (as reported in full-year 2024) - around 65%. This segment should remain supportive and create a baseline for revenues after a prolonged dip. 20% of their revenues come from remodeling and upgrades, which could be hit but remain a cyclical push eventually. The only market that remains most sensitive to the macro is ~15% of revenues, and that comes from new installations. A comparison with data from 2018 shows the trend has not really altered much. The new constructions remained a similar portion of the business. Maintenance was 60%, and refurbishments accounted for a higher 25% share of the business. That implies a scenario of delaying demand, which could return once the macro eases.

Another factor that has made the stagnation (likely to become the longest stagnation in more than a couple of decades if the macro conditions remain unfavorable) look deep and long is the post-COVID demand rush that effectively changed Pool's topline growth rate. Earlier clocking high single-digit growth rates, post-COVID demand saw revenue growth of 25-35% over a couple of years. Tougher comps make the current stagnation look deeper than it would have been if revenue had continued its trajectory at the high single-digit growth levels.

As per the guidance issued in Q3 2025, the full-year sales outlook is flat to slightly up, so the wait for a rebound will continue in the short term. There are some green shoots that appear, though. Management noted a decline in the discretionary spending drag in Q3 2025 compared to a year ago. Digital adoption also reached healthy levels of 17% of sales in Q3, opening up newer and faster ways of reaching the customer.

Competitive Position and Margins Intact

There are other supportive factors for Pool's Buy thesis beyond digital adoption and private labels. Most importantly, Pool's market share and pricing power still indicate its competitive strength is intact. Pool's competitive advantage of a wide network (now digitally augmented) seems to be holding up as usual (even growing in market share terms) despite operating in a business with low barriers to entry and open to competition from regional and big retailers. Management emphasized favorable pricing and market share gains, as well as the support from the maintenance segment, in its Q3 commentary. Its market share position is also intact. According to management commentary in Q3,

..our new pool construction sales have outperformed industry permit data indicating continuous share expansion

The resilience of the maintenance segment and pricing power has meant a 50 bps improvement in gross margins in Q3. Taking out the post-COVID highs of around 31-32%, current gross margins remain overall stable around the ~30% mark. The inventory situation does not appear to be a major drag on pricing power either. The Q3 inventory levels were reported at $1.2b (~4%), lowest levels for the year, after peak season in Q2. The inventory levels were similar a year ago. After a strategic drive to reduce inventory levels and balance product availability, pressures from an outsized inventory look well-handled at the moment - not a scenario where inventory levels are building up visibly (seen from a historical context).

Operating expenses increased by 3%, and overall SG&A grew in mid single digits due to technology investments. These are manageable upticks and should pay off with further digital footprint and a rebound in sales. Looking at the analyst estimates, EPS and revenues are likely to rebound in mid-single digits as early as in 2026. I am not so sure about when the rebound will happen, because even if rates moderate, economic outlook for 2026 is not clear.

Capital Allocation Concerns As Revenue Flat

Repurchases remain supportive and consistent. Even a year ago, Pool had repurchased shares worth $159m and that number is marginally higher at $164m in Q3 2025. The net debt has increased in the meantime from ~$1.15b to ~$1.25b. This is not a significant change in the balance sheet position (for a company reporting $178m operating income even in a flat demand environment), but continued repurchases (with ~$490m still remaining authorized) with no improvement in the balance sheet could be a theme markets will look at. I am not too perturbed by the repurchases and find them supportive, in fact. However, the answer to all of Pool's woes will lie in a demand rebound, when markets will start feeling comfortable around capital allocation. I don't see any panic downturn in the making, so I look at the balance sheet as well within manageable limits.

Valuations Perspectives

Overall, Pool's thesis appears well balanced. Demand fundamentals are under stress, but execution shows no extraordinary inventory buildup or margin contraction. Even revenues have held up decently and look well poised to rebound when demand returns in the long term, given its competitive positioning is intact. The thesis therefore rests on valuations a lot. How much of this stagnancy is priced in?

The EV to forward EBITDA is now at ~15.7x. This is ~23% below its 5-year average and looks reasonably contracted. However, this 5-year period also includes the boom period post-COVID, where Pool was trading at ~30x EV to forward EBITDA. Zooming out and looking at trailing EV to EBITDA metrics, ~16x is somewhere near the bottom of the valuation history. This is often a poor indicator for a value Buy thesis, as markets may continue to keep valuations depressed for long or even correct further.

The reason, I find the valuations fair is the fact that Pool's demand downturn has not been severe enough (suffering also due to tougher post-COVID comps) to materially compress margins. One way to look at valuations is that EBITDA has collapsed by ~40% from the post-COVID highs. EV/EBITDA has also contracted by a similar ~35% range from the post-COVID highs. However, from a longer term lens, valuations are ~35% cheaper than 2019 while EBITDA has grown by almost 80%+.

This is why I view Pool as a long-term Buy. The short-term post-COVID comps are hurting the narrative, particularly with continued demand softening. But when you zoom out, the long -term thesis is intact. Growth may return in 2026 or later, but the way Pool's gross margins have held up and the maintenance segment remains resilient, we should eventually see pent-up demand shoot up at some point for investors with a 2-3 year horizon. Refurbishments uptick is a signal to be watched closely and is generally cyclical, and could only be delayed, not withdrawn.

The primary risk to the thesis is delayed gratification. If demand continues to remain soft, inventory and capital management will continue to be focused on. Comps will start getting easier, though, and competitive position will ensure growth resumption over the next couple of years (or earlier). I see current valuations as a favorable opportunity for fresh entries (with Pool trading at near 5-year lows). I rate Pool a Buy and will accumulate further on dips.

data.title: Truist Financial Corporation: Truly Rewarding, But It Already Had A Good Run
data.description:
It’s been eight months since my initial coverage of Truist Financial Corporation (TFC). It has already increased by 16%, justifying my bullish outlook before. Now, macroeconomic conditions are still volatile. TFC continues to demonstrate its resilience with its solid performance, supported by its prudent asset diversification and loan management. However, valuation is already fully valued in line with my target price, while technicals warrant caution amid neutral market sentiments.

TFC Q3 2025: A Brief Recap

During the third quarter of 2025, the market environment remained challenging as inflation intensified. Meanwhile, the impact of the interest rate cut last year continued to materialize. As a bank, Truist Financial Corporation is highly vulnerable to economic volatility and cyclicality. Yet, TFC assures u of its resilience with its disciplined and strategic asset management.

In the same quarter, its interest income amounted to $6.29B, down by 1.1% YoY from $6.35B. However, we can see its continued rebound from its weak trend in Q4 2024 and Q1 2025. In fact, total interest income rose by 2.1% on a QoQ basis. It was also higher than in my previous coverage by 1.7%. Interest on loans remained the primary component, as it comprised 76.6% of the total amount. Although it weakened YoY, it has already improved since my previous coverage. From there, we can say that TFC has already coped with the impact of the policy easing cycle in 2024. I believe that it benefited from the 100 bps rate cut because it boosted loan demand. It is also worth noting that interest on securities remained sound as it rose by 8.3% YoY and partially offset the weakness in interest on loans and other earning assets.

Meanwhile, its interest expense weakened YoY and stayed generally flat in the past five quarters. After all, the policy rate cut also lowered the interest on deposits and borrowings. It may have also helped maintain asset quality. As a result, net interest income rose by 3.0% QoQ and 1.2% YoY.

Lastly, its non-interest segment remained strong, which should not be surprising as it had more loan demand and deposit inflows. This shows a larger customer/client base. It was also able to manage non-interest expense, which suggests efficiency amid rising prices. As a result, its net income margin remained a double-digit value at 23.1% versus 20.4% QoQ and 22.7% YoY.

Potential Risks

Inflation remains a risk factor for banks like TFC, especially now that it’s back to its 14-month high of 3.0%. It remains intense because prices are still increasing, although slower than in previous years. So, the current outlook remains bleak. Tariffs may amplify it due to their inflationary nature, which can raise import and shipping costs. These extra costs may be passed on to consumers. Amid all these trends, fiscal and monetary decisions may be affected. With that, TFC should be on the watch and be more mindful of its asset management and diversification strategies.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential risks, I believe that TFC remains insulated.

1. Loan Management Is Positioned Well Against Macroeconomic Headwinds. One of the things I like about its loans is its exposure to commercial loans. These comprise 60% of the total amount, while the remaining portion goes to consumer loans and credit cards. This increased a bit from 59% in the previous year. In my view, having a higher concentration of commercial loans is strategic because they are typically more stable and secure than consumer loans. Borrowers are often business owners, so they are more capable of repaying loans and avoiding delinquencies. They are also collateralized, making them more secure than consumer loans. In addition, consumer loans are asset-backed since they are mostly mortgages, home equity, and auto loans. Asset quality can speak for itself. As you can see, nonperforming loans remained low and flat YoY at 0.48%. Loans that are 30-89 days past due decreased to 0.54% versus 0.57% QoQ and 0.58% YoY. This proves the positive impact of having higher commercial loan exposure.

For its loan split, 54% of the total have variable rates. To be honest, this is one of my concerns since variable-rate loans are subject to lower repricing rates. Note that there has been a 50-bps rate cut in the past two months. This can lower yields further. However, this can boost loan demand since it means lower borrowing costs. It can attract more borrowers and even offset lower yields through lending and credit card fees. The remaining 46% are fixed-rate loans, so TFC can lock in higher margins here. Yields are more manageable with decreasing funding costs amid the policy easing.

2. It’s Flexible Due To Its Asset Diversification Strategies. For a typical bank, loans may comprise about 80% of the total interest-earning assets. Yet, TFC shows more balance because of its reliance on other interest-earning assets like investment securities and deposits. Loans only comprise 66% of the total, while 27% goes to investment securities. Its deposits comprise 7%, while the remaining portion comes from other interest-earning assets. This allows TFC to have more flexible and balanced revenue streams. This also helps it manage risk exposure. So if one interest-earning asset weakens, the other one can cover it. We can see how interest on investment securities offset the decrease in loan and deposit interests.

3. It Remains Well-Capitalized. TFC’s highest-quality capital or CET1 Ratio is 11.0%. This will cover the same portion of risk-weighted assets. If we include the other Tier 1 and the remaining capital, the total capital ratio will increase to 14.2%. This is much higher than the Basel III requirement of 8.0%. This means that TFC can withstand financial crises or recessions and stay liquid even without borrowing. This can help it limit deposit run risks and remain operational even if it happens.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation using price ratios, the price uptrend in previous months has been justified. However, they suggest limited upside because TFC is almost fully valued today. In P/B, the stock trades at 0.99x its book value per share versus the five-year average of 1.03x. But given their gap, the price increase may become limited. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $48.18. To prove this, I also included P/S. As you can see, the stock trades at 3.29x its sales per share versus the average of 3.37x. This leads to a TP of $47.44. With that, entering at its current price may generate lower profits than in my previous coverage.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. I based its annualized dividend on the quarterly payout of $0.52. For its dividend growth rate, it’s 2.93% based on the 2020-2025 CAGR. But I decided to round it off to 3.00%. Lastly, I estimated its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP of $46.22. This is in line with my TPs using price ratios.

Technicals

TFC’s technicals indicate neutral sentiments despite recent rebound attempts. The price moves above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, suggesting a stronger trend than in the past 50 or 200 days. However, it’s important to note the strong selling volume before the market closed yesterday. It just offset the midday rally, so sellers and/or profit-takers are still there to take advantage of some upside. The 50-day SMA also proves the observation due to its flat trend.

Meanwhile, MACD still shows that bulls still dominate the market. The MACD line is still moving above the signal line, while the histogram remains within positive territory. This means that buying volume is still higher than selling volume. But note that the histogram is very close to zero or the neutral zone, which implies that momentum is not exactly strong. In addition, RSI is already above 60, which is already near the overbought region. RSI also explains why momentum still suggests caution because it may prompt profit-takers again.

Conclusion

Truist Financial Corporation remains robust despite macroeconomic volatility. Its strategic loan and investment management ensures yield stability and liquidity. However, valuation appears to be at its limit as the stock becomes almost fully valued. Technicals show some rebound attempts but still warrant some caution. I am not selling, but I won’t add more positions, so I suggest a hold rating.

data.title: Tecogen: Huge Demand, Tiny Company
data.description:
Tecogen Inc. (TGEN) is poised for growth due to increased data center demand for its natural gas chillers, which cost less than half of adding new power generation. As a small company, even modest data center orders could significantly impact its revenue. With a strong cash position and a factory capable of ramping up production, it is a compelling growth story that the market typically values very positively.

The Company

TGEN designs, manufactures, markets, and maintains cogeneration products like heat and power generators, chillers, air conditioners, and water heaters for multi-family residential, commercial, recreational, and industrial use in the United States. It also operates distributed generation electricity systems under long-term sales agreements. Its machines are cost-efficient, aiming to deliver energy savings and resiliency from power outages. Its combined heat and power cogeneration system uses natural gas to generate electricity, and the waste heat is used to heat the building’s water, resulting in a more cost-efficient system in comparison with using the utility grid, according to its 10-K 2025.

The company is benefiting from the increased demand from data centers for its cooling solutions. On the Q3 2025 earnings call, management mentioned:

“The level of interest has ramped up substantially. We're getting interest from well-known colocation data center developers. We have now presented our solution to NVIDIA, AMD, and hyperscale developers. Across the board, the feedback has been positive.”

And:

“What is more exciting is that we have started to attract the attention of big-name developers who are well-established in the industry, including those with multiple AI data centers already constructed or in construction.”

Not only that, but management also said that many of the larger data centers that are using on-site power generation are partially idle due to a short supply of gas turbines, which is helping to increase interest in TGEN chillers as an alternative. Because of this short supply, gas turbine costs increased substantially, and Tecogen's chillers are now less than half the price of installing new power generation equipment with equivalent capacity, which is great news for the company. As a solution to this data energy bottleneck, data centers are switching to natural-gas-fired cogeneration systems. In a press release, the company highlighted the benefits of its natural gas chillers, which included lower long-term operating costs and up to 30% savings on electricity costs, which could then be redirected to computing power. Overall, the demand is there, and the company is already seeing revenue improvements from data center demand.

Latest Results

Looking at year-over-year results in the Q3 2025 income statement. The chiller segment increased substantially, from $37,979 to $2.2 million. The cogeneration segment had a decrease in YoY revenue, from $1.2 million to $0.5 million, but in a nine-month period, the cogeneration segment still increased 22%, from $2.1 million to $2.6 million, resulting in a total product revenue increase of about 114% YoY, and, in a nine-month period, product revenue increased 189%, from $3 million to $8.6 million. The services segment, which tends to be more stable and recurring, saw revenue increase by only 2.4%, indicating that the company had some difficulty passing through inflationary costs. In the Energy production segment, revenue fell 34.2% to $255,816 in Q3 2025, due to expired contracts and site repairs. This resulted in a YoY total revenue growth of 27.5%, from $5.6 million to $7.2 million.

On the cost side, the company was impacted by higher labor and material costs, both in the products and services segments. Cost of sales rose 58.8% to $5.0 million. This reduced the gross margin to 30.4%, down from 44.1%. Management noted on the Q3 earnings call that these were the costs of improving chillers with new engines to make them more robust and to decrease maintenance costs in the future, but also to accelerate the chillers' validation process, so that they can be marketed and sold to data center companies. Operating expenses increased 27.7%, from $3.3 million to $4.2 million, largely due to increased payroll and travel expenses. This resulted in a net loss of $2.1 million, which is more than double the $0.9 million loss last year, but reflects the commitment to product improvement so it can serve data center demand. Going forward, the company can count on a strengthened balance sheet after raising about $18.1 million through an equity offering, which increased its cash position from $5.4 million to $15.2 million, while the total debt is only $2.8 million.

Valuation

Another important hint management gave on the last earnings call was: “We're definitely getting told across the board that on-site power generation is substantially more expensive than choosing our chiller option.”

We can use the above information and compare Tecogen with companies in the on-site power generation business.

For this comparison, I am adding the following companies: Vistra Corp. (VST), which is planning to expand nuclear, gas, and coal operations to provide electricity for data centers; Talen Energy (TLN), which is acquiring existing plants for data center contracts; NRG Energy (NRG), which acquired 13 GW of gas-powered generation targeting data center contracts; Constellation Energy (CEG), which has contracts with Meta and Microsoft to provide electricity for data centers; IREN Ltd. (IREN), a vertically integrated data center, owns and operates electrical infrastructure and data centers; and Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD), which is making liquid and air cooling systems for data centers.

The company does not have positive earnings. So, looking at metrics like price/sales, EV/sales, and price-to-book, the company is being valued slightly above peers.
