However, that is for a very good reason. The market is pricing significant growth, and the company is still tiny. For example, a 200-megawatt datacenter can use 200 chillers, while the company is accustomed to delivering around 10 chillers per year, depending on the demand. Also consider that after chillers are delivered, the company still earns from long-term maintenance contracts, which for data centers will also enjoy reduced travel costs, since the chillers will be in the same space, around the data center. Additionally, management said in the Q2 earnings call that once current supply-chain bottlenecks are resolved, the company should be able to manufacture 40 to 60 chillers per year without needing any factory upgrades. But with “minor factory modifications,” the company could increase production up to 100 chillers annually. This increased production could push product revenue toward $50 million, and total revenue could reach $80 million in a few years.

Risk

As every investment has risks, I believe the company incurs two most important risks. First, to scale up production, the company depends on its suppliers being able to increase their own output, and they may not be able to do so, or at least not at the level the company needs. Second, an “AI-bubble pop” has the potential to stop some data center projects and reduce demand for the company’s chillers. However, I view this risk as a minor one, since the TGEN is a small company, and even a single small data center could generate meaningful demand for its products.

Conclusion

This is not a new story, since the stock has already jumped more than 600% in less than a year, but it is a very good story, and the market usually reacts positively to that. If the company announces improvements in its chillers, improvements in its supply chain, or more orders from data centers, which I think is very possible, I believe the stock may still see some upside, which is why I view the stock as a “buy.”

data.title: Nuvation Bio: A Strong Company Now Fairly Priced
data.description:
Background

I began building my position in Nuvation Bio (NUVB) earlier this summer at roughly $2.50 per share. Back then, I concluded the market severely underpriced the value of Ibtrozi, an agent treating ROS1-positive NSCLC, and assigned almost no value to the rest of the pipeline of the Company. I was so confident that I submitted this article to the Best Garp Idea writing competition of SA on September 15, 2025.

After another quarter worth of Ibtrozi rollout data became available, on November 7, 2025, I wrote a follow-up piece, entitled Ibtrozis Rollout So Far Confirms Bullish Case. By then, the stock had climbed to $5.00.

I did not expect the stock price to increase so quickly to today’s $8.00 or so. The visibility of Ibtrozi’s commercial rollout has certainly improved with the release of yet another quarter's new patient data. However, the stock has now climbed to a level that more than fully reflects such improved visibility. For that reason, I am moving my rating from BUY to HOLD (with nuances below).

Current State of the Business

Ibtrozi’s commercial rollout is off to a strong start, and accelerating. The most recent quarter saw approximately 204 new patients beginning the therapy, or about 3 new patient each business day, as reported by the company during the 2025 Q3 quarterly earning call. This marks meaningful acceleration from the 70 new patients for the 34-business-day period between June 12, 2025 and July 31, 2025, or roughly 2 new patient each business day, as reported in its Q2 2025 earning call.

Assuming no meaningful frontloading of prescriptions and no slowdown of uptake for the foreseeable future, that implies roughly 800 new annualized new patient starts. Using a rather conservative net price in the range of $180,000 -$230,000 per patient per year, this translates into an early revenue run rate around $145–185 million per 12 rolling months.

What makes the microeconomics of the company of the next at least 3-4 years so attractive is the long duration of progression-free response—about 50 months for the patients to Ibtroz in clinical trials. This means that patients on average can be expected to remain on treatment for slightly over 4 years, and the “stacking” effect becomes obvious.

Even if the company merely holds its current pace of new patient uptake, total patients on therapy could reach roughly 1,600 during the second full year of launch and expand past 2,000, possibly toward 3,000, the year after. Under the same uptake and per therapy price assumptions, these figures support a conservative forward revenue range of roughly $300–$600 million.

The company’s financial position remains solid. With more than half a billion dollars in cash and marketable securities, Nuvation has no immediate dilution risk and certainly no liquidity concerns.

Why A Downgrade from BUY to HOLD

The primary reason for this downgrade is straightforward: its stock price. When I accumulated shares in the $2–$3 range, investors were effectively paying for the net cash position of the company, as the pipeline was "free" then. Back then, the short sellers were pushing the narrative that the ROS1-positive NSCLC market is too small (3,000–4,000 newly diagnosed patients each year) and too crowded with three preexisting approved drugs had kept the valuation suppressed.

That disconnect has mostly closed—likely aided by ironically by the short covering as Ibtrozi's early rollout data so far have crushed such narrative. The rising tide of better valuation for life science stocks overall recently has probably also helped me.

If one models a realistic steady-state revenue estimate of roughly $300-600 million over the next several years and applies conventional oncology valuation frameworks—multiples of about 3x to 5x peak sales—the current market cap of the company of about $2.7 billion no longer reflect a meaningful discount. It reflects reasonable expectations, plus some modest value for the pipeline.

This does not mean the stock cannot rise further. It simply means the wide margin of safety that justified aggressive buying at $2.50 is no longer present. Additional appreciation will require either faster U.S. adoption, earlier-than-expected ex-U.S. contributions, or meaningful pipeline validation. I do not claim any further scientific insight into those issues. Such outcomes may or may not happen, but they are no longer severely underpriced by the current stock price.

Risks to My Thesis

Although the stock is now fairly valued on the current information, long-term investors must acknowledge the possibility that the company continues to outperform. If new patient starts increase substantially—say, if the company begins capturing more than half of newly diagnosed ROS1-positive small lung cancer cases—the stacking effect becomes significantly stronger. In that scenario, today’s valuation could prove conservative, and investors waiting for a pullback may simply miss the next leg higher.

International markets introduce additional upside. The global ROS1-positive population is several multiples of the U.S. market. If the company’s ex-U.S. partners execute well, commercial expansion outside the United States could contribute substantially to the price of the stock than currently priced in.

There is also the pipeline of clinical stage drug candidates. Although today’s valuation, or at least stock price movement, is driven almost entirely by Ibtrozi, any progress from the developmental programs could shift the narrative about the company and its stock quickly and dramatically. For investors who decline to add at today’s price, the opportunity cost could be substantial.

Commercial Risks and Competitive Pressure

Despite the strong start, Ibtrozi’s rollout is still in its early innings. Early enthusiasm sometimes stabilizes as prescribers settle into a new treatment choice. The company must continue expanding prescriber adoption while competing against existing and emerging therapies in a small, crowded niche.

Real-world tolerability patterns can occasionally diverge from clinical data. While Ibtrozi currently enjoys favorable comparisons, the competitive landscape is not frozen. Nuvalent Inc (NUVL), for example, may receive FDA approval for its drug candidate in the same indication. Even if a competitor is not clinically superior, additional entrants can slow momentum by dividing prescriber preference or affecting competition dynamics.

In short, while the company has executed well, we only have two quarters of commercial data of Ibtrozi. Those risks now matter far more at $8.00 per share than it did at $2.50 per share.

What Existing Shareholders May Do

For investors who bought near the lower range, continuing to hold may make sense to the extent the investors have the requisite knowledge to assess the future performance of Ibtrozi and the pipeline of the company, and the confidence that the company will continue to execute well, so long as its long-term prospects remain intact.

For investors who do not have such knowledge or long term horizon, and wish to deploy capital to more attractive opportunities elsewhere, trimming is also reasonable now that the valuation is no longer deeply discounted.

For those who hope to take advantage of the rapid rise in the stock price while keeping their core position, selling covered calls at around $10.00 may be worth considering. This allows investors to collect premium while retaining shares unless the stock price exceeds the strike price, in which event the investors have to be ready to part with the shares. If the stock rallies sharply—for example, well above $10.00—the shares may be called away at $10.00, so anyone pursuing this strategy must be comfortable with that possibility.

If the stock stays below the strike price, the investor keeps both the shares and the premium from the sold covered calls. However, if the stock price declines dramatically, the investor may be better off selling the shares directly at today's price.

The assumption here is that the investor will be comfortable holding the shares for the long term if the price declines, but also comfortable selling at around $10.00 in the short term.

Conclusion

Nuvation Bio remains a fundamentally strong company: a differentiated FDA- approved ROS1-positive NSCLC agent with a promising if not fully established commercial trajectory, a proven management team, and a robust balance sheet.

My downgrade to HOLD reflects its current valuation after the substantial price appreciation during the last 5 months or so, not a loss of confidence of its business fundamentals.

I will revisit this rating as additional commercial, clinical or other data points emerge.

data.title: Via Transportation: Underlying Fundamentals Remain Sound
data.description:
Investment Action

I had a buy rating for Via Transportation (VIA) previously, as the business addresses a large TAM, which it should get incrementally better at capturing demand due to its data moat and efficient GTM strategy. I reiterate my buy rating for VIA, as the recent results were very solid, with government demand holding up and new customer wins accelerating. While the market seems to be fixated on temporary weakness, I see the current share price as an attractive entry point.

Q3 2025 Earnings Overview

VIA’s first quarter as a public company played out even better than I expected. Q3 revenue grew 32% y/y to $109.7 million, and the growth came mainly from the core platform rather than any one-off tailwinds. Platform annual run-rate revenue has also reached $439 million, up 32% y/y, which tells us that it is scaling nicely. Demand was also coming from the right customers. Government revenue grew 34% y/y, and the US business grew 42% y/y. Margins also moved in the right direction. While adj. gross margin only expanded 40 bps y/y to 39.6%; there was very strong operating leverage seen as adj. EBITDA margin improved to –7.9%, a 920 bps expansion from –17.1% a year ago. This shows us that VIA has a visible path to profitability as revenue scales.

The Viral GTM Strategy Is Working Very Well

As a recap, my view on VIA’s GTM strategy was that as it wins one influential city and proves the model works, nearby regional adoption should follow through as relevant planning bodies gain confidence in VIA’s offerings. I cited the Austin example as solid proof of concept, but with the latest Q3 data, we now have even more concrete data that this works nationally. To be exact, we are now seeing the Austin playbook happening in new geographies.

Specifically, a new cluster has started to form in the Midwest, as VIA has gone live with Omaha shortly after its win at the neighboring city of Council Bluffs. Michigan is also showing the same pattern (as seen from platform revenue increasing). This is meaningful because it proves VIA’s model works even in other regions. Perhaps a stronger data point is that VIA added 24 net new customers in Q3, up sharply from the 7 added last quarter. My view is that this jump in new logo adds is the clearest signal that the viral GTM strategy effect is real and replicable, not a one-off win tied to Austin.

Competitive Displacement

I have also grown more confident about VIA’s moat. The trigger here is VIA’s success in securing the Mobile, Alabama contract by directly replacing a legacy vendor, which I see as a massive deal. The contract is worth ~$60 million over five years, structured as a three-year agreement with two one-year renewals, and, very importantly, the city plans to shift more capabilities from the incumbent vendor onto VIA’s platform. For readers that have not dealt with municipal clients before, they are often slow in decision-making (too much bureaucracy) and generally don’t churn after they have deployed a large IT project. For them to be willing to rip out an existing system (that they have used for a long time) and rebuild operations around VIA, it really shows how substantial the efficiency gains are. My view is that they must have substantially outweighed the friction of rip-and-replace in order to justify that action.

I would think this makes the path ahead for VIA to win deals from other regions even clearer, as peer validation (i.e., reputation) is very important in this industry. My belief is that agencies rarely want to be first to adopt “new solutions” (why take the risk?), but they are comfortable being second or third once a comparable city has proven the switch is safe. Like I said before, the more VIA scales, the better it becomes. For recap, my general idea is that as VIA scales, it gains more real-time data, and at the same time, it embeds itself deeper into day-to-day operations. The latter dramatically increases switching costs and makes it harder for peers to displace it.

Why The Negatives Are Actually Bullish Indicators

In contrast to my view on the recent results, the market perceived it negatively, and that has sent the share price down to the current level of ~$34. Based on my analysis, there are two metrics that investors seem fixated on. First, it is the y/y decline in Net New ARR dollars, and second, it's the flat ARR per customer. If you are bearish on VIA, these data points would read as demand weakness. However, I see the opposite. I actually think that both of these metrics should get better in the coming quarters, and the current “weakness” is temporary.

On Net New ARR, yes, the dollar figure was lower than last year, but looking at that number in a vacuum is not the right way to assess it. If you consider velocity in new logo adds, which VIA added 24 net new customers in Q3 (a record for the business and more than triple the 7 added last quarter), it makes sense because these government contracts take place in phases. Or simply, VIA is signing contracts faster than the deployments can be activated. So while Net New ARR is down, remember Net New ARR doesn’t include dollars from backlog, and that backlog should show up in ARR as these cities ramp up deployment. I would be concerned if both logos and ARR dollars were falling, but that’s not the case here. Therefore, treating a single quarter’s ARR dip as a structural slowdown doesn’t make sense to me.

With ARR per customer, let's be fair here. The metric dipped by ~1% sequentially, and bears are calling this proof of weak upsells. What this tells me is that VIA is still at the “land” part of its land-and-expand model. They are signing school districts and smaller municipalities, which naturally come in with smaller initial deal sizes than a major metro system. Obviously, when you add a bunch of smaller customers into the base, the average gets pulled down. The right metric to assess upsell is net revenue retention, which is still solidly above 120%. This also tells you what happens after that: VIA signed its first contract, and the customer continued to spend >20% more every year.

Therefore, to me, the slight dip in ARR per customer isn’t a red flag. It’s just math; a higher denominator with lower-value customers drives down the average, but this is exactly how you build the installed base that leads to predictable long-term growth (via expansions).

Valuation

I truly think the market is misunderstanding the dynamics around VIA’s ARR metrics. It is clear to me that demand remains very strong, GTM motion (land-and-expand and viral marketing) is working, and upsell momentum continues (as seen from the retention rate). What VIA has to do in the coming quarters is to simply show that those ARR metrics recover and continue to grow revenue, which can compound at >20%. When these happen, I believe a sharp re-rating should follow. I am sticking to my previous viewpoint that VIA should at least trade higher than Tyler Technologies (TYL) (currently at 7.6x forward revenue multiple) given the higher growth potential (TYL is only expected to grow at ~10% for the next two years).

Risk

Government contracts are naturally lumpy, and this volatility may lead to volatile quarters for net new ARR and customer adds. As we have seen in Q3, even when the broader demand trend is healthy, the market is not forgiving. Additionally, the recent wave of smaller customers only pays off if VIA can layer on higher-margin software over time. If upsell momentum stalls, net revenue retention could dip lower.

Conclusion

My conviction on VIA hasn’t changed. I am still buy-rated. Demand is healthy, the viral GTM model is working, and the recent noise in ARR metrics looks temporary rather than structural. As VIA onboards its record number of new customers and upsell momentum continues, I believe growth can compound at >20% for several years.

data.title: DouYu: Time To Turn Cautiously Optimistic
data.description:
DouYu (DOYU), a live streaming platform in China for games and e-sports, progressed in some areas, but it also regressed in other areas in its Q3 FY2025 report, which was released on November 20. On the one hand, the top line shrank, a continuation of what has happened a lot in recent years, but, on the other hand, profitability went up, unlike the losses of the past. Still, DOYU has arguably shown enough to be cautiously optimistic that long DOYU has enough to become a winning play.

DOYU has yet to move out of consolidation

A previous article from August 2025 written by myself mentioned how DOYU was able to power the stock much higher, but also at great cost because it was accomplished by paying special dividends. However, this tactic caused a vast reduction in what used to be substantial cash reserves, and there was not much more room on the balance sheet for this to continue. The stock stalled, and DOYU was in need of another catalyst to pick up the baton and power the stock higher. This catalyst had yet to present itself, and I therefore opted to rate DOYU a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the stock is trading below where it was three months ago. Nonetheless, the stock remains in the consolidation phase following a rally earlier in 2025, which builds on the rally in the stock that started in April of 2024. This rally was powered by DOYU announcing special dividends totaling a combined $19.70 per ADS, which cost DOYU about $600M in cash. DOYU also announced a reverse stock split during this time.

The last dividend to the tune of $9.94 per ADS was paid out shortly before the stock peaked at $9.93 per ADS in March 2025, but with no more dividends announced since then, the stock has been left without the catalyst that powered it higher for almost a whole year. Not surprisingly, DOYU has essentially gone sideways since then by moving within a range.

However, if we connect the lows during this time, then it becomes clear the stock is currently close to the lower end of the range, as shown in the chart. Note, for instance, how the stock has been hovering close to the trendline in recent days. The trend has been for the stock to move within the range, and if we assume this trend remains intact, then the stock is likely to trade back towards the upper half, if not the top end, of the range next. If someone is looking to get in on DOYU and in need of an entry point, then now is likely to be a good time, close to the trend.

Why there is reason for cautious optimism after the latest report

The stock has been rather muted in recent days, including following the release of the Q3 FY2025 report. The report might have gotten a better reception, but while there was some notable progress to be found, the report also makes it clear that there is more that needs to be fixed. For instance, average mobile MAUs declined by 27.5% YoY to 30.5M in Q3 FY2025, down from 42.1M in Q3 FY2024. As a result, revenue declined by 15.4% YoY to RMB899.1M, or $126.3M, in Q3 FY2025, down from RMB1,063.1M in Q3 FY2024.

On the other hand, DOYU was able to counter the decrease in the top line with an increase in the bottom line. DOYU achieved this by lowering the cost of revenue, including by reducing revenue-sharing fees and with a reduction in content costs. DOYU also decreased operating expenses, which resulted in income from operations reaching RMB11.93M, or $1.68M, in Q3 FY2025, which is much better than the loss of RMB94.18M in Q3 FY2024.

Net income attributable to ordinary shareholders increased by 233.2% YoY to RMB11.33M, or $1.59M, in Q3 FY2025, up from RMB3.4M in Q3 FY2024 and that is with Q3 FY2024 benefiting from much higher non-operating income, mainly because of there being far more cash on the books a year ago and the interest income that generates. In contrast, Q3 FY2025 saw much less interest income because there was much less cash on the books, with a lot used to pay special dividends.

On a per-share basis, DOYU earned RMB0.38 or $0.05 per ADS in Q3 FY2025, up from RMB0.11 in Q3 FY2024. In terms of non-GAAP, net income was RMB23.1M, or $3.3M, in Q3 FY2025, up from a net loss of RMB39.8M in Q3 FY2024. This converts to a net income of RMB0.77 or $0.11 per ADS in Q3 FY2025, up from a net loss of RMB1.32 in Q3 FY2024.

It’s true DOYU is still in the red in terms of TTM earnings, but the trend is positive. For instance, non-GAAP EPADS totaled minus $1.63 TTM, but this includes a loss of $2.59 in Q4 FY2024. If we were to exclude the $2.59, then earnings would flip into the black at $0.96. This would equate to a P/E ratio of 7.4x with the stock last closing at $7.14 per ADS.

It’s only one quarter, but the substantial improvement in earnings is an encouraging sign. If DOYU can supplement the current earnings growth with a lasting return to growth in revenue, then that would put an end to the contraction that troubled DOYU in the last several years, following the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Why DOYU is arguably undervalued according to at least one metric

Assuming earnings are on the rise and remain so, then this has significant ramifications in terms of how DOYU ought to be valued as an investment. For instance, the balance sheet has cash, cash equivalents, and short-term bank deposits totaling $294.88M, which is actually more than what DOYU is valued at with a market cap of $215M and the stock closing at $7.14 per ADS on November 26.

There is some debt of about $1.4M, but there is also $14.04M in long-term bank deposits and another $55.73M of investments to account for. If we add everything up, DOYU has a book value of $281.5M with total assets of $436.36M and total liabilities of $154.86M, which converts to a book value of $9.33 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is 30,178K.

Not only is DOYU available at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.76x, but it is also available at less than the cash DOYU has access to according to the books. Such a low valuation was perhaps in order at a time when DOYU was posting net losses and book value was at risk of being reduced as a result, but this looks out of order now that earnings are on the rise.

DOYU has to stay out of the red, but if we assume DOYU manages to achieve this and book value is no longer at risk of decreasing because of, for example, negative free cash flow, then an argument can be made that DOYU is undervalued based on the balance sheet. If book value is going up and not down, then DOYU deserves a fair value of at least equal to book value, or $9.33 per ADS, and well above where the stock last closed at.

What could change the bull thesis for DOYU

There is one wild card out there that could torpedo the case for long DOYU. If the number of users on the platform keeps dropping, it could reach a point where betting on DOYU becomes too much of a risk. There are many factors that can influence whether people decide to go out to stream their latest, for instance, moves or tactics in multiplayer games, but if long-term DOYU is to remain viable, some stability, and more ideally growth, in the number of users is needed.

COVID-19, and its social restrictions, were the main culprit of the adverse changes DOYU has seen in the past five years. However, it is also worth mentioning that in the past few years the live streaming business has become more competitive with some pretty ambitious providers that are looking for a piece of the pie. DOYU will need to deal with the competition for users.

DOYU has managed to grow earnings by reducing the cost of revenue and by reducing operating expenses, but, in the long run, revenue will need to stop contracting like it did in the Q3 FY2025 report because if this continues, then eventually earnings will start to feel the impact of a reduced top line. To grow revenue, DOYU needs to put a stop to significant reductions in MAUs, like what happened in Q3 FY2025.

However, there is reason to believe DOYU can achieve this. A major reason why DOYU has seen fewer users lately is because there is not enough reason to go out and play due to a dearth of new and interesting games to play. Combine this with other competing options, and this could explain why fewer users showed up.

The good news is that there are new games on the horizon, which should stimulate gamers to go out and live stream their abilities. This should provide a boost to DOYU in terms of MAUs, which should in turn boost revenue to the point it can be expected to return to the path of growth. If DOYU can do this, and if it can grow earnings like it has, much, if not all, of the downsides to long DOYU would go away.

Investor Takeaways

It would not be correct to state DOYU is in the clear or that there is no work to do. DOYU has to return to sustained growth, or at least put an end to contractions, which requires that there are no sustained reductions in the number of users on the platform. In the most recent report, DOYU reported a 27.5% decline in the number of MAUs. This resulted in a 15.4% YoY decline in revenue to RMB899.1M, or $126.3M, in Q3 FY2025. This is something that needs to be addressed because a sustained decline in users will make it hard to invest in a company that could be perceived as on the way out.

However, it’s encouraging to see how, in spite of a decline in revenue, DOYU was able to grow earnings, and by a lot no less. Non-GAAP earnings grew by 233.2% YoY to RMB11.33M, or $1.59M, which bodes well for the future if DOYU succeeds in returning to growing revenue, whether or not as a result of the introduction of new games currently in the pipeline. If earnings can grow with a shrinking top line, then earnings should grow even stronger with an expanding top line.

DOYU has yet to fully recover from the hole it fell in after the pandemic, but it appears to be getting there, if slower than most people would have liked. Still, if we assume DOYU will get better, especially once revenue follows in the footsteps of earnings, then long DOYU is worth taking into consideration. The charts also suggest that now could be a good time to place a bet on a stock that is likely to trade higher.

In addition, DOYU is still available at not just less than book value, but also at less than the cash, and what can be quickly converted to cash, on the books. This may have been warranted when the income statement was in the red, but not with DOYU back to earning profits and with earnings on the rise. If book value is not going down, then DOYU is undervalued at less than book value.

There is more work to do, but I am bullish on DOYU with all the above in mind. The number of MAUs is something that needs to be watched closely, but DOYU also deserves the benefit of the doubt that it can reverse the decline, especially if the decrease is not preventing earnings growth. As long as earnings do not fall into the red, some decline in MAUs can be overlooked.

Bottom line, if DOYU is generating profits, it does not deserve to be valued at less than the cash it can access. At the very least, DOYU should be worth at least equal to the net cash it has access to. This is currently not the case, but history suggests that such a situation usually does not last and will get corrected by the market. Odds are the same will happen in the case of DOYU.

data.title: Black Rock Coffee Bar: Remain Very Positive On The Growth Runway
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Black Rock Coffee Bar (BRCB) previously with a buy rating, as there was a visible growth path ahead, given the whitespace available and good unit economics. BRCB’s Q3 reinforced my bullish view on the stock. It is driving double-digit SSS growth, new stores are performing, and the mix shift into food is lifting the average ticket without relying on price increases. Valuation also screens cheap vs. larger peers that have lower growth.

Q3 2025 Earnings

In my opinion, BRCB's Q3 2025 results validate my view. My thesis was that BRCB will continue to see unit growth and solid unit economics, and Q3 data points proved me right. Total revenue grew 24.2% y/y to $51.5 million, but the most important data point here is the 10.8% same-store sales [SSS] growth. I take this double-digit SSS growth as a solid indicator that the brand is scaling without cannibalizing other stores. The mix shift also moved in the right direction. Food mix expanded 210 bps y/y to 12.6%, showing that BRCB is successfully attaching higher-margin food items to beverage orders. This lifts the average ticket without the need to raise prices organically. BRCB also opened 11 new stores, bringing the total to 169, and notably, the new cohort is outperforming on both sales and store-level profitability. This is another very bullish sign that BRCB store performance can be replicated. As for profitability, store-level profit grew 30.7% to $15.2 million, significantly outpacing revenue growth and expanding margins by 150 bps to ~29.5%. At the consolidated level, adj. EBITDA grew 35.1% y/y to $6.9.

Traffic, Loyalty, And Innovation

The underlying drivers for the 10.8% SSS growth are perhaps even more important than the headline figure. It was driven mostly by transactions (contributed 6.1%), and this distinction (traffic contributed more than pricing) is meaningful because it shows real brand strength. At the very basic level, it shows that customers are visiting more often and that the concept is gaining relevance with consumers. This also makes the overall growth profile more durable since BRCB now has the choice to “give up” pricing to further drive transaction growth. Even if pricing doesn’t grow, SSS growth is still well in the mid-single-digits level.

The loyalty program is another key point to note. Participation has grown to ~64% of transactions, a clear sign that the program is resonating with customers, and the positive impacts are becoming more apparent. We know this is happening because loyalty members spend about $0.70 more per check than non-members. This $0.70 may not seem like a lot, but given that the average price of a BRCB product is in the $4 to $6 range (refer to the menu and try ordering from the stores), it effectively implies a >10% “pricing” uplift. This also directly supports the point on flywheel I made previously. As more guests become loyalty members, the average pricing should naturally increase, and since the program was only relaunched recently, I think we are still early in the adoption cycle.

On product innovation, I really like the move into offering food options. Management specifically highlighted the recent launch of egg bites (in July) as a key reason why food mix increased to 12.6% of sales. I believe this matters a lot because it widens the spectrum of purchase occasions that BRCB can address. Previously, it was only drinks; now, consumers that stop to purchase a beverage could turn into breakfast or snack visits, which increases both ticket and profit dollars per transaction.

Growth Should Accelerate

Q3 performance was certainly great, but I think there is a good chance for growth to accelerate in the next few quarters with the timing of stores opening. In Q3, BRCB opened 11 new stores, but most of them came online late in the quarter because of permitting delays. In other words, these new stores' revenue contribution in Q3 was minimal, and this sets up Q4 to show the full benefit of those 11 units, plus another 10 openings expected by the end of this year.

There are also hints of this happening when we look at the implied Q4 SSS growth guide. Management called for high-single-digit SSS growth for FY2025, which implies an 8–9% SSS growth for Q4. But October already delivered 8.7% (source: Q3 earnings call), and that was against a tough 13.6% comp last October. It is very clear that underlying demand momentum is very strong, and as the comps base eases in November and December (refer to the chart above), SSS growth should be better than October’s 8.7% if SSS growth momentum continues.

Here is another even more interesting aspect to consider. While revenue contribution is minimal, the cost associated with operating these new stores still flows through and, I believe, is higher than revenue contribution because it takes time to ramp the store up the maturity curve. This means that reported EBITDA margins are compressed, and as these stores ramp up and the store opening pacing becomes more balanced (i.e., not all back-end loaded), we should see operating leverage kick in. Also, the fact that management kept its FY25 target of 30 openings despite the permitting delays also shows the pipeline remains intact.

Valuation

With new units ramping, easier comps ahead, and a cleaner store opening cadence in 2026, my view for how the stock will act is to jump during the upcoming earnings quarter (I believe BRCB has a path for beat-and-raise quarters).

Valuation-wise, I think BRCB is still cheap. It trades at ~5.2x forward revenue today with >20% growth potential for the next few years. This compares well to the bigger players like Yum! Brands (YUM) (6x forward revenue) and McDonald’s Corp. (MCD) (~10x forward revenue) that have lesser revenue growth (MCD at low single digits and YUM at low teens). I would think that if BRCB does perform like I expect (beat and raise guidance through the coming quarters), the market should start to re-rate the multiple higher, closer to these larger peers.

Risk

Another key risk is execution. The recent permitting delays show us that store development can be uneven, and that can create short-term revenue swings. The market may not digest this well, and the share price may take a hit if it happens. Competition is also another area to watch as Starbucks (SBUX) launched its “Back to Starbucks” initiative, which may be aggressive in regaining share in several regions. Lastly, while coffee tariffs are manageable today, any escalation or related measures could put more pressure on margins.

Conclusion

I maintain a buy rating on BRCB. The strong SSS momentum, the loyalty ecosystem that is gaining traction, and the positive early results from food innovation give me confidence that there is still a long runway for growth. Trading at ~5.2x forward revenue despite >20% growth potential, I believe the valuation still looks compelling relative to larger peers.

data.title: Alphabet Has Won
data.description:
The AI War

An investing lesson we should all learn from Netflix (NFLX) is that the company had long been the streaming wars winner before we came to realize it. Disney investors know something about it, as the narrative that Disney would be a real competitor and a threat to Netflix held until, at least, 2022. What did Netflix realize before anyone else? I think we could summarize in this way: the company realized that streaming wasn't just about having some movies to distribute, but it was about integrating movie originals with a proprietary distribution (via streaming) system.

Now, as early as this may sound, I want to argue that the AI war is already over and that Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) has won. I would like to show how the company has pulled ahead in the race to AI leadership in a way that the recent developments already reflect it. There are some strong arguments that I want to make and that I am following as I am currently investing in Google and increasing my stake, believing that once the winner is identified, profits will come for many years. Even though I am not fond of backing a bull case with a superinvestor, I think we would have never seen Berkshire Hathaway step in if the management there didn't think of a long-term play. But I think Buffett's last move makes much more sense than what many other superinvestors have been doing, reducing and trimming their Google holdings seemingly because they think the stock has run too much.

Things indeed change fast. A year and a half ago, many thought Alphabet would go out of business. And yet, the fundamentals weren't lying back then, just as they do right now, suggesting what I want to argue regarding Alphabet's future. Now, everyone wants to own Google, but we need to understand whether we are moved by FOMO or a thorough understanding of Alphabet's position for many years to come.

What The Market Thought At First

When OpenAI (OPENAI) and Microsoft released ChatGPT, we heard Satya Nadella saying that they had made Alphabet dance. Soon afterwards, Alphabet released Bard, and we probably remember that it wasn't a success, presenting some obvious flaws. Just a few days ago, Marc Benioff's post on X went viral as he defined the launch of Gemini 3 as a jaw-dropping (euphemism) moment that made him not want to switch back to ChatGPT. What has happened in just two years?

Let's then analyze step by step the bull case I want to share.

Alphabet's Starting Point

I assume most of us know Alphabet. For completion's sake, let's quickly recap that it has three main businesses: Google Services (Search, YouTube, Gmail, Chrome, Android, Google Play) that makes money mainly via selling ads and then through subscriptions (for example, YouTube Premium) and device sales; Google Cloud, which makes money through subscriptions and usage fees; and Other Bets (Waymo is the prominent one), which is still losing money but that could soon become a massive third engine. To make things even easier for every reader, allow me to show below the last quarterly revenues by segment, together with the 9M results. We see that Alphabet had its first-ever $100B quarter and that it could easily end this year above the $400B landmark.

I won't deal with Waymo here (the whole segment "only" loses $3.9B as of Q3, meaning that if Waymo alone could lead to profitability in a couple of years), even though I think the case for Alphabet's leadership in autonomous driving and robotaxis is so strong that I have often argued how, until a couple of months ago, when we were buying Google shares, we were basically grabbing hold of extremely high-quality businesses while getting Google Search for free. I have also debated that if robotaxis are the new frontier, either Tesla or Google was completely mispriced. I think Waymo will also integrate perfectly into the new AI-driven ecosystem Alphabet is building. But we will have time to deepen our understanding of this topic in future articles. Now, besides these three businesses, Alphabet also talks about Alphabet-level activities, which comprise some AI-focused R&D activities that are quite meaningful. In fact, this is where Alphabet accounts for the development costs of its AI models. Peanuts, isn't it?

So, the first key consideration is that Alphabet's starting point in the AI-race is already years ahead of its competitors because it begins with a fully integrated ecosystem where Gemini is now the newcomer that is being embedded to add new compute power and drive improved monetization across the full stack.

What I would like to highlight is Google Cloud. Until a few years ago, it was the Cinderella of the "Big Three", trailing behind AWS and Azure, both because it was unprofitable and because it was at a smaller scale. Right now, even though it is smaller than the other two, it is no longer a story of investors hoping it will do well. It has become extremely profitable, and it is growing at 30+% rates. Its backlog grew 46% QoQ to $155B: Alphabet's prominence in AI is heating its own cloud more than we would have thought.

The Distribution Advantage

Depending on what data provider you use, you will see that Google Search's share hovers between 90% and 93%, with Bing only having 3%-4%. In addition, when we look at the browser market share, we see that Chrome is gaining traction and has increased its share by over 5 percentage points in just two months, from August 2025 to October 2025. In the mobile OS, Android has a market share of around 73%.

What does this imply? Let me state it brutally: Google doesn't need to beg for traffic. To defeat this starting point, you have to build a very great model that is truly unique. Otherwise, Google sits in front of the large majority of the market and can therefore benefit from economics that no one can compare to. OpenAI, backed by Microsoft, did make Google dance, but both companies combined don't have the distribution monopoly that Alphabet has seized.

By the way, I think that one of the reasons why Alphabet was at first "late" to the party was mainly because of its massive scale. A small and unknown company such as OpenAI could easily take its chances once its LLM started working reasonably well. But Google has to be more prudent: in case something goes wrong, billions of people are affected. Therefore, its scale necessarily demanded a step-by-step approach. Now we are obviously realizing that Google was ready for the race. In early November, for example, it finally released the integration of Gemini Deep Research with Workspace content. Was there someone not expecting it? All of us, and perhaps we were wondering why it was taking so long. Well, the reason is that this integration allows Deep Research to gather precise information from our personal workspace. This is a delicate task. Now, we all know that Google already has full visibility on what goes in and out of our email accounts. But Google's prudent developments allow it to make things work seamlessly. At stake is Google's reputation, which can't be put easily at peril just because people want Google to hurry up.

As of now, we know that ChatGPT has around 700M weekly active users, with almost 200M daily users. Gemini is behind with 650M monthly users and around 35M daily users. But Gemini 3 and the Deep Research integration are surely set to rapidly increase these numbers, as we will see Alphabet benefiting from its massive scale. We also know that over 70% of Google Cloud's customers use AI and that 13M developers are using Alphabet's generative models. As Sundar Pichai recently wrote, this is "just a snippet of the impact" that Google is seeing.

Now, when you add on top of that Gemini 3 is among the best, if not the best, model available and that it quickly caught up with ChatGPT, there are fewer and fewer reasons for Google users to switch from Gemini, which they now have fully integrated with their favorite apps, to other LLMs.

Since Gemini 3 was released, several reports have come out, one of the most interesting ones is from Wharton School professor Ethan Mollick, who concisely takes us through the 1,000-day journey from the launch of ChatGPT to Gemini 3, showing how we are truly close to Ph.D.-level intelligence. Whether Gemini 3 or ChatGPT 5.1 is better is up for debate, but my point here is different. Alphabet wouldn't need to be much better than OpenAI (although, of course, it wants to be the technological winner): as long as it has something that is at least comparable to OpenAI, Alphabet benefits from its massive distribution advantage, which will probably lead to rapidly increasing usage numbers quarter after quarter. Remember, every tech war so far has first been about reaching the tipping point, after which network effects do the rest. This race sees Alphabet better positioned because when the company pushed an update, it immediately reached billions of people, while competitors such as OpenAI, Perplexity, and Anthropic have to work harder for each new download of their app.

AI As A Commodity Favors Alphabet

This advantage has one important consequence. If Alphabet has the advantage of instant scale, it can make its AI a utility rather than the end-destination of a process. By this, I mean that the so-called commoditization of AI is a further advantage for a company such as Google that has already built an ecosystem where users have found a comfort zone. Allow me an example. When smartphones were released, for some time, hotels differentiated themselves because some immediately offered free Wi-Fi and USB charging ports for phones. For a few years, some people chose their hotel based on these "perks". But then these features became commodities that we find pretty much everywhere (in my town, widely known for its tourism exposure, it is a common joke among hotel directors that customers, before even asking for their room number, want the Wi-Fi password). The fact that AI will probably become a commodity plays in Alphabet's favor.

Three years ago, the narrative was that Google was being exposed to an existential threat that would wipe it away, just like we have seen happen with Kodak or Nokia. Today, it is hard to argue in favor of this take, as data suggests that Google has, at least, been able to counter the first blow, switching then to attack mode.

Ironically, three years ago, investors were worried about the DoJ investigating Alphabet for its dominance in search. Some even thought the judge would force Alphabet to divest from Chrome. Then ChatGPT was released, and this was part of the argument that saved Google: the search landscape was rapidly changing, and it was hard to determine whether Google would still be dominant. Google was saved, investors were relieved, and, in the meantime, Chrome's market share has accelerated.

Probably, the next frontier of AI will be video rather than text. I think it is obvious that Alphabet, since it owns YouTube, has by far the best library to legally train its models on proprietary data. This explains why we often hear that, while GPT 5.1 is a text-heavy model, Gemini is better at multimodal understanding, because it is trained to process both video and audio better than its competitors.

Full-stack AI

Alphabet also has the advantage of a full-stack approach, which spans LLMs, distribution platforms, and, most importantly, AI infrastructure. This is where Alphabet has, as far as I see it, only one true competitor: Amazon (AMZN). In fact, just like Amazon, Google is also disengaging itself from dependence on NVIDIA's chips. Google designs its own TPUs (Tensor Processing Units), which offer the perfect combination of high-performance and cost-efficient inference, since they are designed for neural networks. Because of the way they are designed, TPUs focus only on AI-related operations (GPUs were born for computer graphics and are more general-purpose oriented). Therefore, throughput is higher and consumes less energy. However, TPUs are less flexible than GPUs, as they clearly are designed to be perfectly integrated into Google's ecosystem.

This makes a big difference because it allows Google to train its models at a lower cost per query versus those competitors that have to rely on H100/Blackwell GPUs designed and built by NVIDIA. Compute right now is a big bottleneck, especially because of its cost. Google has a cost advantage, and the proof is easily shown. For ChatGPT, people have to pay a subscription, which is right now OpenAI's main revenue source. On the other hand, Alphabet can bundle its AI in its ecosystem and give it away for free (more on this in the next paragraph).

Right now, Alphabet can be seen as a new alternative to Nvidia, which is why we are hearing of talks with Meta of a 500k TPUs deal that analysts believe could boost Alphabet's earnings by up to 3%, since the purchase would be worth billions. Now, we have to get this straight, because on this news, Nvidia lost around $150B in market cap. TPUs don't directly threaten Nvidia's business. Even though Alphabet can sell them, they are not conceived as a product; they are rather part of the larger project to achieve best-in-class unit economics.

However, Google's TPUs could create another advantage. Right now, NVIDIA's GPUs are the standard and are then the "commodity". This means that companies can swap from AWS to Azure because they are compatible. Let's then imagine a startup that wants to lower its cost structure. It would turn to Google's TPUs. But if a company modifies its code to run on TPUs because it has a clear cost advantage, it is then locked into Google Cloud, without the possibility of interchanging between the other major cloud providers.

Moreover, when we talk about AI, we need to keep in mind that there is a big difference between training and inference (this is why Amazon has Trainium and Inferentia). The former happens once, but the latter is the process that happens once the model is trained. Therefore, inferences happen billions (and maybe trillions) of times a day. TPUs won't kill NVIDIA, but they might actually be a threat to OpenAI because they allow Google to charge less (or even give AI away "for free"). In this case, Google's aim is not to sell chips, but to have cheap compute so that competitors can be undercut in price.

Alphabet's Business Model Wins

Since I have been arguing for a long time that a big consequence of the AI revolution is the creation of a massive energy bull case, I want to use the example of the gasoline supply chain to explain the cost advantage Google has.

To drive your car, you need energy, which comes from gasoline that releases power when it is burned. AI's gasoline is processing power, also called compute. Every time we write a prompt in a chatbox, we are requiring compute, and we truly burn energy. Now, to have gasoline at the gas station, you need a company that drills and refines oil and a company that buys it to sell it at a gas station. We could compare NVIDIA to the company that digs for oil and refines it, since it makes the chips. These are then sold to Microsoft (at a huge gross profit margin), which is similar to a gas station that buys gas from its supplier. Microsoft buys compute and then sells it to whoever needs it, just like a gas station serves any car driving in. OpenAI is the car that comes and needs gas. Clearly, the retail customers pay a retail price for gas. Now, it is true that OpenAI has unique partnerships with the other two companies, which probably allow it to have good deals on compute costs. But it has to go through two markups, just like many other AI companies.

On the other hand, Google has vertical control of the whole supply chain and doesn't pay any markup.

We could even use another comparison, although I don't want to stretch it too far. Let's go back to Netflix, since this analogy could provide some food for thought. The old cable bundle was the outcome of simultaneous partnerships. Microsoft/OpenAI/NVIDIA could represent this old business model: Microsoft offers the cloud, OpenAI has the large language model, and NVIDIA builds the chips. Google, on the other hand, is more similar to Netflix. It builds its own TPUs, it has its own data centers, has a proprietary model, and it even owns the end screen by controlling billions of devices via Android.

The in-house advantage that Alphabet has built allows it to have computing power at a cost that is 20% of the price paid by end-users who need to use NVIDIA's GPUs. This is why many are talking about the so-called "Nvidia Tax". And guess who, besides Alphabet, is also trying to free itself from Nvidia's lethal kiss? Amazon. A cost structure that is 20% of the costs other competitors have to face creates a very strong advantage that supports my overall bull case.

Let me add one consideration. Have you ever thought about Gmail's price? Some may ask me, "What are you talking about? Gmail is free". Well, it actually has a price: $0.00. This is actually predatory pricing because it is obviously below cost, preventing new companies from really challenging Google's dominance in email accounts. Why is it sustainable? Google locks more people within its ecosystem, which is then monetized through ads and subscriptions. Google could do the same with Gemini. OpenAI simply can't unless it wants to be a non-profit foundation that always finds external funds (I am a bit exaggerating, but you get the point).

From a business model perspective, bundling wins over subscription due to its vertical integration advantage.

Monetizing AI

For a hyperscaler, the issue of monetizing AI is easily solved: selling infrastructure utilization is the solution. This is why I say that cloud providers are the new utilities. Moreover, Google benefits from consumption economics, which has a multiplier effect. When a company spends money to obtain AI compute from Google's TPUs, it often has to spend on storage, BigQuery, and networking. If a company uploads its database to train a model on the cloud, the data is somewhat trapped and very hard to move to a different peer. Data exit costs, which is why we hear people talking about "data gravity". Google can also charge subscription fees for Gemini Pro or other future upgrades. This fee becomes almost full profit (except for taxes) because once Google has paid its fixed costs, computing becomes extremely cheap. So, Google's AI monetization model is three-faced: it has a consumption-based model, it benefits from the multiplier effect, and it charges subscription fees. This means that Google basically needs people to use its AI to spin the meter, as a true utility. It needs volume. And the reality is that it has all the volume it wants.

Conclusion

Winning the AI race is not about who has the smartest chatbot but about having the best business model that can be sustained over the long term. With Google, AI is no longer a product, but another feature of the larger internet experience we have daily. Once we realize that this is the business model with the highest likelihood of success, it becomes easier to invest more in Alphabet and profit from any future dips.
